content.problemStatement:
“You can make a lot by playing such games.”

Every player has to play a game of Ddakji with the recruiter in order to enter the squid game. Gi-Hun has played many rounds but was unable to win even once. The recruiter thus decided to give him a puzzle instead.

Gi-Hun is given an array AAA of NNN elements.
He can perform the following operation on array AAA any number of times:


Select any two indexes 1≤i,j≤N1 \leq i, j \leq N1≤i,j≤N such that i≠ji \neq ji=j and replace AiA_iAi​ with Ai−AjA_i - A_jAi​−Aj​.
His task is to find the minimum possible value of ∣A1∣+∣A2∣+…+∣AN∣|A_1| + |A_2| + \ldots + |A_N|∣A1​∣+∣A2​∣+…+∣AN​∣ attainable by performing these operations several (possibly, 000) times.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains NNN, the number of elements in the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers — A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each testcase, print on a new line one integer — minimum possible value of ∣A1∣+∣A2∣+…+∣AN∣|A_1| + |A_2| + \ldots + |A_N|∣A1​∣+∣A2​∣+…+∣AN​∣.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with an integer N, the size of the array.
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
All values fit in 64‑bit signed integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible value of |A1|+|A2|+…+|AN| after performing any number of allowed operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: Perform the operation with i=1i = 1i=1 and j=2j = 2j=2 four times. The array is now [0,1][0, 1][0,1], and its value is ∣0∣+∣1∣=1|0| + |1| = 1∣0∣+∣1∣=1.
Test Case 2: No operations need to be performed.
Test Case 4: Perform the following operations:

Choose i=4,j=2i = 4, j = 2i=4,j=2 and set A4=6−2=4A_4 = 6 - 2 = 4A4​=6−2=4. The array is now [0,2,−2,4][0, 2, -2, 4][0,2,−2,4].
Choose i=4,j=2i = 4, j = 2i=4,j=2 and set A4=4−2=2A_4 = 4 - 2 = 2A4​=4−2=2. The array is now [0,2,−2,2][0, 2, -2, 2][0,2,−2,2].
Choose i=2,j=4i = 2, j = 4i=2,j=4 and set A2=2−2=0A_2 = 2 - 2 = 0A2​=2−2=0. The array is now [0,0,−2,2][0, 0, -2, 2][0,0,−2,2].

The value of the array is now ∣0∣+∣0∣+∣−2∣+∣2∣=4|0| + |0| + |-2| + |2| = 4∣0∣+∣0∣+∣−2∣+∣2∣=4, and it can be proved that this is the minimum possible value.

solution.approach:
The operation A_i = A_i - A_j does not change the greatest common divisor (gcd) of the whole array because gcd(a‑b, b) = gcd(a, b). Hence the gcd g of the absolute values of the initial array is an invariant and every element after any sequence of operations will be a multiple of g.

If g = 0 (all numbers are zero) the answer is 0.
Otherwise we consider the signs of the original numbers (ignoring zeros). Two cases arise:
1. All non‑zero numbers have the same sign (all ≥0 or all ≤0). Using the Euclidean algorithm we can make N‑1 elements zero and keep a single element equal to ±g. The sum of absolute values then equals g.
2. There are both positive and negative numbers. In this situation at least one positive and one negative non‑zero element are required to keep the overall gcd equal to g. We can transform the array into exactly two non‑zero elements: g and –g, the rest zero. The minimal sum is therefore |g|+|‑g| = 2·g.

Thus the answer is:
- 0 if g = 0
- g if the array contains numbers of only one non‑zero sign
- 2·g otherwise.

The algorithm per test case:
* Compute g = gcd(|A1|, |A2|, …, |AN|).
* Detect whether there exists a positive element and a negative element.
* Output according to the rule above.
The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over the whole input.
